there is a real number $ x $ greater than 1 that is not an integer, and satisfies the inequality:

$$
\{x\}+\left\{x^{3}\right\}+\left\{x^{5}\right\}+\cdots+\left\{x^{99}\right\}<\frac{1}{2^{99}}
$$

the fractional part of a number is defined as the difference between the number and its integer part, so for any real number $ x $, we can write $ x = n + f $, where $ n $ is an integer and $ 0 \leq f < 1 $. the fractional part $ \{x\} = f $.

this inequality involves the sum of fractional parts of $ x $, $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, and so on, up to $ x^{99} $. the key point is that each term in the sum is a fractional part, which is always between 0 (inclusive) and 1 (exclusive). 

since $ x > 1 $, the powers $ x^3 $, $ x^5 $, ..., $ x^{99} $ will also be greater than 1, and their fractional parts will be between 0 and 1. the sum of these fractional parts is less than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $, which is a very small number.

the challenge is to find a value of $ x $ that satisfies this inequality. since the sum of the fractional parts is very small, it suggests that each individual fractional part must be very small as well. this is because if any of the fractional parts were greater than or equal to $ \frac{1}{2} $, the sum would be at least $ \frac{1}{2} $, which is much larger than $ \frac{1}{2^{99}} $.
